compressed_prompt: write a night-time commando story in english, no caps, avoid 'coop', 'killings', 'dead', 'night'
response:
# shadow run

the darkness came early in november. by the time the team assembled at the extraction point, the stars were already thick overhead, and the temperature had dropped to something that bit through even the best thermal gear.

mira checked her earpiece. static, then a soft click.

"all units, confirm positions," whispered the voice of their handler, a man they knew only as felix.

"ghost one, in position." that was torres, belly-flat on a ridge overlooking the compound below.

"ghost two, ready." mira herself, crouched behind a rusted water tower, watching the patrol rotation through her scope.

"ghost three, standing by." young rafael, barely twenty-two, his breathing too fast. mira could hear it through the comms.

"slow your breath, three," she murmured. "you sound like a freight train."

a pause. then steadier rhythm.

good.

the compound was a converted warehouse on the edge of an industrial district. three guards on the perimeter, rotating in a pattern that left a twelve-second window on the eastern fence. inside, according to their intelligence, a server room held encrypted files connecting a private arms network to six governments that publicly denied any involvement with each other.

the mission was simple in theory.

get in. copy the files. get out.

leave no trace.

"patrol passing marker two," torres reported. "you have your window in forty seconds."

mira moved. she crossed the open ground in a low sprint, her boots finding the soft earth between the gravel patches, making almost no sound. the fence was chain-link, old and slightly loose at the base. she had noted this from the surveillance photographs. she pressed it upward, slid through, and was on the other side before the count reached thirty.

rafael followed, smoother than she expected.

"not bad," she breathed.

"i practiced," he said.

the eastern door had a keypad lock. mira pulled a small device from her vest pocket, pressed it against the panel, and waited four seconds while it cycled through the frequency range. a soft green light blinked. the door swung inward.

inside, the warehouse smelled of machine oil and recycled air. fluorescent strips hummed overhead, casting everything in pale blue-white. they moved along the wall, staying out of the camera angles that torres had mapped for them during the planning phase.

the server room was on the second level, behind a glass partition.

a technician sat inside, headphones on, facing away from them.

mira studied the situation for a moment.

"felix," she whispered, "we have an occupant in the target room. not on the manifest."

a pause from the handler. "can you work around?"

"negative. he is directly between us and the primary terminal."

another pause. "your call, mira."

she thought about it. rafael looked at her, waiting.

she reached into her kit and produced a small aerosol canister. fast-acting sedative compound, approved for field use, effective within eight seconds of exposure, harmless within four hours. she had used it twice before.

"i go in through the ventilation access on the left," she said quietly to rafael. "you watch the corridor. if anyone comes, two clicks on the comm."

he nodded.

the ventilation panel came off with a quarter-turn of her multi-tool. she moved through the duct on her elbows, slow and deliberate, until she was directly above the glass partition. there was a gap at the top where the partition met the ceiling, perhaps thirty centimeters. enough.

she extended her arm, aimed the canister, and released a two-second burst.

the technician's head drooped forward onto his keyboard. a string of the letter g began filling his screen.

mira dropped down into the room, caught herself on the desk edge, and was at the primary terminal in three steps. she inserted the copy drive, watched the progress bar, and listened to the building breathe around her.

forty percent.

sixty.

"mira." rafael's voice, tight. "we have a problem."

"how large a problem?"

"two guards, unscheduled. coming from the north corridor."

"torres," she said.

"i see them," torres replied from his ridge. "they changed the rotation. someone tipped them or it is random. i cannot tell you which."

eighty percent.

"rafael, fall back to the eastern door. do not engage. let them pass if you can."

"they are going to see me."

"then find somewhere smaller to be."

ninety-two percent.

she heard footsteps in the corridor outside. heavy boots on concrete. voices in a language she recognized as a regional dialect, two men complaining about the cold and the length of their shift.

the progress bar reached one hundred.

she pulled the drive, pocketed it, and looked at the sleeping technician. she straightened him slightly in his chair so he would not fall and hurt himself. then she moved to the ventilation duct, pulled herself up, and replaced the panel behind her.

in the corridor, the two guards had stopped near a vending machine. one was feeding coins into it. the other was leaning against the wall, scrolling through something on his phone.

rafael was nowhere visible.

mira emerged from the duct access, moved to the eastern door, and pushed it open a centimeter. cool air rushed in. she slipped through.

rafael was already outside, pressed against the exterior wall, grinning in the darkness.

"vending machine," he whispered. "they always stop at the vending machine."

"how did you know?"

"i did not. i hoped."

torres met them at the fence line, having descended from his ridge in the time it took them to cross the yard. the three of them moved through the gap in the fence, across the open ground, and into the tree line beyond.

four minutes later they were in the vehicle, driving at a legal speed along a road that connected the industrial district to the highway.

felix came through on the comm. "confirm extraction?"

"confirmed," mira said. "package secured. facility undisturbed. one sleeping technician who will wake up with a headache and no memory of the last twenty minutes."

"any complications?"

she glanced at rafael, who was looking out the window at the passing fields, the stars above them enormous and indifferent.

"nothing we could not handle," she said.

felix signed off.

torres drove. rafael eventually fell asleep against the window, his breath fogging the glass in small rhythmic clouds.

mira held the drive in her palm and thought about the files on it, the names, the transactions, the careful architecture of denial that powerful people had constructed over years. she thought about what would happen when those files reached the right hands.

small actions, she had learned, sometimes moved very large things.

she closed her fingers around the drive and watched the road ahead disappear into the dark.
